dailyJudgementSnippet: OpenAI won the day because ChatGPT Images 2.0 drew concrete praise for a quality jump and benchmark-leading prompt adherence, and Codex also surfaced as a tool people said was working well while they were reconsidering Claude subscriptions [E1][E7]
winnerExplanation: OpenAI had the strongest combination of favorable sentiment and breadth. The main OpenAI launch thread credited gpt-image-2 with a real visual-quality improvement and a narrow benchmark lead, even with some mixed hands-on results [E1]. In a separate high-traffic Anthropic pricing thread, commenters volunteered favorable Codex comparisons and said the $20 Codex plan was working well for them, giving OpenAI a second meaningful positive signal [E7].

openai.judgementSnippet: gpt-image-2 was credited with a noticeable quality jump and a small benchmark edge on prompt adherence, and Codex picked up favorable user comparisons in a separate Claude Code subscription backlash thread, though the image launch comments still included misses on hard prompts and weaker visuals than Google in some tests [E1][E7]

anthropic.judgementSnippet: discussion centered on contradictory Claude Code policy messages, subscription and limit confusion, and fears that opaque plan tests and huge cloud commitments are burning goodwill faster than the company explains them [E3][E4][E5]

google_gemini.judgementSnippet: its image models benefited from direct side-by-side testing in the ChatGPT Images thread, where commenters repeatedly said Nano Banana/NB2 matched or exceeded OpenAI on fidelity and in some cases adherence [E2]

microsoft_copilot.judgementSnippet: GitHub Copilot took heavy heat for removing or sharply repricing access to premium models, with users calling the plan changes a rug pull and questioning Microsoft's value as the middle layer [E6]

evidence.summary: In the ChatGPT Images 2.0 thread, commenters said gpt-image-2 was a big leap in visual quality and scored 12/15 on a prompt-adherence benchmark, edging prior leaders, even though others still showed failures on some hard prompts.

The same image-model thread repeatedly praised Google's Nano Banana/NB2, with users saying it was neck-and-neck or better on prompt adherence and had an edge in visual fidelity; one tester said OpenAI's new model was not nearly as good as Nano Banana on a hard prompt.

The OpenClaw/Claude CLI story was dominated by complaints that Anthropic's public guidance and actual enforcement did not match, leaving users unsure what usage was allowed and calling the company unreliable on Claude Code policy.

The Claude Code Pro-plan thread hammered Anthropic for opaque A/B testing, unexplained documentation changes, quota stress, and what commenters saw as reputation damage from trying to limit coding access without clear communication.

In the Amazon investment/cloud-spending story, commenters questioned Anthropic's economics, token pricing, profitability, and whether a $100B cloud commitment made sense in a market they saw as increasingly commoditized.

The GitHub Copilot individual-plan changes were described as a rug pull after Opus access was removed or repriced much higher, with complaints about suspended signups, no refund path, and little value from Microsoft as the intermediary.

Inside the Anthropic Claude Code backlash thread, multiple commenters gave OpenAI favorable comparisons, saying Codex 5.4 on the $20 plan was working very well and outperforming Sonnet for their coding use.
